coredata.dc:title: Consolidating CQDs with NiFe-MOF photo-Fenton membranes to effectively treat wastewater containing antibiotics and antibiotic-resistant bacteria

coredata.dc:description: The escalating presence of antibiotics and antibiotic-resistant bacteria in wastewater poses a pressing threat to both human health and ecosystems. Addressing this challenge requires innovative approaches while photocatalytic membranes emerge as promising solutions because they offer multiple functions to separate and degrade/sterilize antibiotics and antibiotic-resistant bacteria simultaneously. In this study, we for the first time developed in-situ grown Ni-Fe metal-organic framework/carbon quantum dot (NiFe-MOF/CQD) membranes with NiFe-MOF seeds pre-integrated into the substrates. The incorporation of CQDs not only enhances the hydrophilicity and integrity of NiFe-MOF membranes that results in both a high water flux of 119.7 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 and a high antibiotic rejection of 98.1% to rifampicin, but also promotes the photo-Fenton activity by enhancing the visible light absorption and photoelectrons (e−)-holes (h+) separation. Therefore, the degradation efficiency of rifampicin in a photo-Fenton system within 100 min by NiFe-MOF/CQD composites (84.9%) surpasses that of NiFe-MOFs (63.1%). Additionally, NiFe-MOF/CQDs can effectively inactivate both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria in the photo-Fenton system. The supreme separation performance of NiFe-MOF/CQD membranes and photo-Fenton activity of NiFe-MOF/CQDs underscore the significant potentials of the newly invented photocatalytic membranes in treating wastewater containing antibiotic and antibiotic-resistant bacteria.

head.ce:title: Consolidating CQDs with NiFe-MOF photo-Fenton membranes to effectively treat wastewater containing antibiotics and antibiotic-resistant bacteria

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The escalating presence of antibiotics and antibiotic-resistant bacteria in wastewater poses a pressing threat to both human health and ecosystems. Addressing this challenge requires innovative approaches while photocatalytic membranes emerge as promising solutions because they offer multiple functions to separate and degrade/sterilize antibiotics and antibiotic-resistant bacteria simultaneously. In this study, we for the first time developed in-situ grown Ni-Fe metal-organic framework/carbon quantum dot (NiFe-MOF/CQD) membranes with NiFe-MOF seeds pre-integrated into the substrates. The incorporation of CQDs not only enhances the hydrophilicity and integrity of NiFe-MOF membranes that results in both a high water flux of 119.7 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 and a high antibiotic rejection of 98.1% to rifampicin, but also promotes the photo-Fenton activity by enhancing the visible light absorption and photoelectrons (e−)-holes (h+) separation. Therefore, the degradation efficiency of rifampicin in a photo-Fenton system within 100 min by NiFe-MOF/CQD composites (84.9%) surpasses that of NiFe-MOFs (63.1%). Additionally, NiFe-MOF/CQDs can effectively inactivate both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria in the photo-Fenton system. The supreme separation performance of NiFe-MOF/CQD membranes and photo-Fenton activity of NiFe-MOF/CQDs underscore the significant potentials of the newly invented photocatalytic membranes in treating wastewater containing antibiotic and antibiotic-resistant bacteria.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Water resources are among the most precious natural resources on earth. With the rapid development of industrialization and urbanization, the demand for freshwater continues to increase. However, water pollution has gradually become a serious challenge to fulfil this fresh water demand, as well as threatening global ecological balance and human health [1–3]. Among the numerous factors contributing to water pollution, antibiotic pollution has emerged as a concerning issue, drawing widespread attention [4,5]. Antibiotic pollution primarily stems from activities such as healthcare, agriculture, and aquaculture that leads to the widespread presence of antibiotic residues in water bodies [6–9]. This poses a severe threat to aquatic ecosystems and human health, as antibiotic residues not only disturb the ecological balance of aquatic organisms but may also result in greater antibiotic resistance in those “superbugs” which are difficult to treat with existing medications, exacerbating public health issues [10,11].

To effectively address pollution issues derived from antibiotics, various technologies for removing antibiotics and antibiotic-resistant bacteria have been explored. Anaerobic membrane bioreactors [12,13], adsorption [14,15], electrochemistry [16,17], advanced oxidation [18,19], and other methods [20] have been proposed and investigated. These methods, while exhibiting high processing efficiency, exhibit disadvantages such as high energy consumption or being prone to secondary pollution and pose challenges in terms of practicality [21]. Membrane technology, favored for its efficiency, simplicity, low energy consumption, and relatively low cost, has been extensively studied in the last decade and emerged as an alternative for the removal of antibiotic and antibiotic-resistant bacteria from wastewater in recent years [20]. Zhao et al. enhanced the surface charge intensity of nanofiltration membranes by cross-linking (polyetherimide) PEI on the membrane surface, utilizing the Donnan exclusion mechanism to improve the removal of antibiotics such as cefalexin and enrofloxacin [22]. Guo et al. introduced zwitterions during the interfacial polymerization process to prepare nanofiltration membranes, which exhibit high antibiotic rejection rates and low salt rejection rates for the separation of antibiotics and salts [23]. Other researchers have used layer-by-layer self-assembly to prepare selective polyelectrolyte multilayer (PEM) nanofiltration membranes for the removal of antibiotics from wastewater [24]. These prepared nanofiltration membranes may face issues such as the generation of concentrated pollutants during the membrane separation process [25]. To address this problem, the idea of combining membrane filtration and advanced oxidation processes (AOP) has been raised.

The photo-Fenton reaction, as an AOP, combines the advantages of photocatalysis and Fenton catalysis to degrade a wide range of pollutants in aqueous systems [21,26]. The membranes incorporated with catalysts possessing photo-Fenton activity can combine the filtration process with photo-Fenton based AOP reactions. On one hand, they can separate and simultaneously degrade/sterilize the antibiotics and antibiotic-resistant bacteria to avoid the secondary pollution [27]; on the other hand, as the catalysts are immobilized on the membrane surfaces, no recovery of photocatalysts is needed. In addition, the concentration and enrichment function of the membrane filtration process can increase the pollutant concentration on the membrane surface where the photocatalysts locate, thus improving the catalytic efficiency [28,29]. Therefore, they exhibit synergistic advantages of good treatment effect and continuous operability, holding significant application prospects in the field of wastewater treatment containing antibiotics and antibiotic-resistant bacteria [30,31].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), characterized by highly porous structures, rich tunability, and good compatibility with organic polymers, providing ideal characteristics of membranes such as high permeance, selectivity, and flexibility [32–35]. Among them, Fe-MOFs exhibit excellent stability and hierarchical mesoporous structures [36,37], while Ni-MOFs are also widely used as adsorbents [38]. Moreover, the performance of MOFs can be enhanced by combining them into bimetallic MOFs which may demonstrate a significant synergistic effect [39]. For example, bimetallic NiFe-MOFs, commonly used in electrocatalysis, outperform both Ni-MOFs and Fe-MOFs in terms of adsorption and catalytic performance [40,41]. Additionally, they contain a certain amount of Fe3+ that can undergo the photo-Fenton reactions under light illumination to stimulate the generation of electrons, promote the conversion of Fe3+ to Fe2+, and accelerate the photocatalytic degradation and decomposition of pollutants [42].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have garnered considerable attention recently due to their ultra-small sizes, rich active sites, and excellent biocompatibility [43,44]. Combining CQDs with other materials to form composite membranes can also further enhance membrane permeance by taking advantages of their hydrophilicity and rich terminal groups as bridging agents [45]. Moreover, CQDs have exhibited outstanding capabilities in terms of visible light absorption and charge carrier transport which can effectively suppress the recombination of photo-induced electrons and holes, thus enabling the MOFs to achieve higher photocatalytic degradation performance under visible light irradiation [46,47]. However, whether CQDs can promote the separation performance and photo-Fenton activity of NiFe-MOF membranes to treat wastewater containing antibiotics and antibiotic resistant bacterial has not been studied yet.

Therefore, in this study, NiFe-MOF/CQD composite membranes were in situ grown on the surface of NiFe-MOFs seeded PVDF substrates. PVDF substrates were chosen because of their high chemical resistance, high temperature resistance, and ease of manufacture [48,49]. Rifampicin and tetracycline with different molecular weights and chemistry structures were used as representing antibiotics to evaluate the effectiveness of the NiFe-MOF/CQD membranes. Results showed that the newly developed NiFe-MOF/CQD membranes demonstrated high permeance (119.7 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) and antibiotic rejection rates (98.1% for rifampicin), as well as a high selectivity between salts and antibiotics. Additionally, NiFe-MOF/CQDs exhibited fast antibiotic degradation and effective inactivation of antibiotic-resistant bacteria through the photo-Fenton processes.

Ferric nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, ≥98.5%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent), nickel(II) nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, ≥98.5%, Tianjin Dingshengxin Chemical Reagent), terephthalic acid (1,4-H2BDC, ≥99%, Chengdu Huaxia Chemical Reagent), citric acid (>99.5%, Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology), triethylamine (TEA, 98%, Shanghai Macklin Biochemical), N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMAC, ≥98.5%, Guangdong Guanghua Science Reagent), N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP, ≥98.5%, Guangdong Guanghua Science Reagent), ethanol (≥99%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent), sodium chloride (NaCl, ≥99.5%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, ≥98%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent), magnesium sulphate (MgSO4, ≥99%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, ≥99%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC, 98%, Shanghai Meryer Biochemical Technology Reagent), rifampin (Rif, 98%, Shanghai Boer Chemical Reagent), peptone form fish (≥98%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent), beef powder (Nanjing Quanlong Biochemical), agar (molecular weight: 3000–9000 g mol−1, Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemcial Research Institute), Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) strains (Shanghai Microbiological Culture Collection Co., Ltd., China), polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF, molecular weight: 50,000 g mol−1, Solvay), and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, 98%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) were all used as received. Deionized (DI) water (resistivity: 15 MΩ cm) was obtained from a Milli-Q integral unit (Millipore).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NiFe-MOF seeds and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In a standard synthesis procedure of NiFe-MOF seeds, 1.2 mmol of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (348 mg) and 0.36 mmol of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (88 mg) were blended in 70 mL of DI water and magnetically stirred for 0.5 h to form Solution A. Concurrently, 0.6 mmol of 1,4-H2BDC (99.6 mg) was mixed in 70 mL of DMAC and stirred for 0.5 h as Solution B. Subsequently, Solution A was introduced into Solution B and underwent stirring for 15 min. Then 3.2 mL of TEA was rapidly injected into the mixed solution, which was then continuously ultrasonicated for 30 min under ambient conditions. Finally, the resultant NiFe-MOF seeds were collected through centrifugation, underwent three washes with ethanol, and were finally dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h [50]. To synthesize CQDs, the process began by grinding citric acid into powders and placing them in a glass container. Subsequently, the white powders were transformed into yellow gels after being heated at 180 °C for 3 h, which were then dissolved in water, purified using a dialysis membrane (2 K MWCO), and finally obtained as CQD powders through freeze-drying [46].

The process to fabricate NiFe-MOF/CQD membranes is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 . PVDF substrates with/without NiFe-MOF seeds were first fabricated by an immersed phase inversion process. Then, 9 g PVDF, 0.2 g PVP and pre-determined amount of NiFe-MOF seeds (i.e., 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, or 0.2 g) were added into a 60 mL NMP solution and stirred for 12 h. The resultant mixture was maintained stable at room temperature to remove bubbles, which was afterwards slowly poured onto a clean flat glass plate, scraped out by a casting knife with a thickness of 200 μm, and then immersed horizontally in DI water in a water coagulation bath for phase inversion. After the membrane was completely peeled off from the glass plate, it was replaced in another container filled with DI water for 12 h. Finally, the membrane was washed again to remove the residual solvents. The obtained substrates are abbreviated as SPVDF-x (x represents the mass of NiFe-MOF seeds, measured in grams) while the pristine PVDF substrates without MOF seeds are designated as the control membranes.

The PVDF substrates with or without MOF seeds were then placed in a glass culture dish containing a mixture of A and B solutions with the top surface facing up. After subjecting it to different reaction times in a 60 °C oven, M-SPVDF-x/y membranes (where y represents the reaction time in hours) membranes with NiFe-MOFs grown on the surface were obtained. The preparation of the membranes with surface-grown NiFe-MOF/CQDs was similar to that of M-SPVDF-x/y except adding CQDs to the Solution B. Notably, when the mass ratios of CQDs to NiFe-MOF seeds in the PVDF casting solution were 10:1 and 100:1, they were respectively named as MC-SPVDF-0.15/6(10:1), MC-SPVDF-0.15/8(10:1), MC-SPVDF-0.15/6(100:1), and MC-SPVDF-0.15/8(100:1). For example, MC-SPVDF-0.15/6(10:1) are NiFe-MOF/CQD membranes grown on PVDF substrates with 0.15 g NiFe-MOF seeds in the substrate for 6 h and the ratio of CQDs to NiFe-MOF seeds in the PVDF casting solution was 10:1. The NiFe-MOF/CQD particles were collected by centrifugation of the solutions from the culture dishes after the reaction and being washed three times with ethanol for further characterizations and photo-Fenton activity analyses.

The morphology of membrane surfaces and cross-sections was analyzed using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, GeminiSEM 300). Prior to scanning, all dried membrane samples were coated with a thin layer of gold under standard high vacuum conditions. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of NiFe-MOFs, CQDs and their composites were obtained by a JEM-2100F with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The chemical properties of membranes were investigated using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Nexus 670) within the frequency range of 4000–400 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses were performed using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE instrument with Cu K-α (λ = 0.154 nm) as the radiation source. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted by an Escalab 250Xi instrument to quantify the elemental composition of the membrane surface and analyze chemical bonds. Surface charge properties of membrane surfaces were analyzed using a zeta potential analyzer (Zeta 90 Plus) in a 1.5 mM KCl aqueous solution with a pH range of 3–10. Water contact angles (WCAs) of membrane surfaces were measured using a Biolin Scientific Attention Theta Flow instrument. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2 − for NiFe-MOFs, CQDs, and NiFe-MOF/CQDs were recorded on a Bruker A300. Their ability to harvest visible light was investigated and compared by UV–vis diffusion reflectance spectra (DRS, Agilent Cary 5000).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of separation performance of membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The separation performance of membranes was evaluated by a standard dead-end filtration device with an effective filtration area of 12.56 cm2. To achieve stable pure water permeance (PWP), each fresh membrane was pressurized with DI water in the filtration device under a pressure of 0.5 bar for half an hour, followed by measuring the PWP at 0.5 bar for an additional half hour. Subsequently, evaluations of molecular separation performance were carried out using a 10 mg L−1 tetracycline solution and a 50 mg L−1 rifampin solution. The membrane permeance was calculated using the following Eq. (1):
(1)
PWP =
V
Δ P × A × t
where PWP is the water permeance of the membrane (L m−2 h−1 bar−1), V (L) is the solution volume permeated through the membrane, A (m2) is the effective filtration area, t (h) is the filtration time, and ΔP (bar) is the operating pressure. The retention rate of a certain solute was determined using the following Eq. (2):
(2)
R = ( 1 -
C p
C f
) × 100 %
where R is the rejection, and Cf and Cp are the concentrations of molecules in the feed and permeated solutions, respectively. The concentrations of NaCl, MgCl2, MgSO4, and Na2SO4 were determined by measuring the conductivity of solutions using a conductivity meter (Rex DDS-307A). As for antibiotics, their concentrations were measured using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (TU-1810). The absorption wavelengths of the antibiotics used were 355 nm for tetracycline hydrochloride and 475 nm for rifampicin.

The separation performance of MC-SPVDF membranes for antibiotics from different salts was investigated using a mixture solution comprising 50 mg L−1 of rifampicin and four different salts (NaCl, MgCl2, MgSO4, and Na2SO4 at 1 g L−1 each) as the feedstock. As previously described, evaluation of membranes’ selectivity and permeance was conducted. The selectivity factor as defined in Eq. (3) was employed to elucidate the membrane's desalination performance.
(3)
S =
1 - R
Salt
1 - R
Rif
where RSalt and RRif refer to the rejections of salts and rifampin, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of photo-Fenton activity to degrade antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: 20 mg of the as-synthesized NiFe-MOFs or NiFe-MOF/CQDs were added into a rifampicin solution (50 mg L−1, 200 mL). Prior to visible light irradiation, the suspension was gently magnetically stirred in dark for 30 min to establish the adsorption/desorption equilibrium between rifampicin in the bulk solution and on the materials at room temperature. A 300 W Xenon lamp with a cutoff filter (λ > 400 nm) was used as the visible light source to irradiate the mixed solutions. Subsequently, the Fenton reaction was initiated by introducing 2 mL of H2O2 (30 wt%) into the solution. During the reaction, 5 mL of the solution was withdrawn at regular intervals to determine the rifampicin concentration by measuring its absorbance using the UV–visible spectrophotometer

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of the photo-Fenton activity against bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial properties of NiFe-MOFs and NiFe-MOF/CQDs were evaluated using the spread plate method with E. coli and S. aureus respectively selected as the model Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. The specific procedures were as follows: First, the glassware and other related materials to be used were autoclaved at a high temperature to prevent interference from other bacteria. Next, the solid culture medium and physiological saline (i.e., the solid culture medium composed of 0.5 g peptone, 0.3 g beef extract, 0.5 g NaCl, 1.5 g agar, and 100 mL distilled water) were prepared at pH = 7.0. Then, E. coli and S. aureus cultured for 24 h were inoculated separately into 100 mL of physiological saline. Afterwards, 20 mg of NiFe-MOFs and NiFe-MOF/CQDs were dispersed in 100 mL of physiological saline containing the inoculated bacteria. The reaction was allowed to proceed in dark for 30 min to reach the adsorption/desorption equilibrium. Then, 200 μL of H2O2 (30 wt%) was added into the reaction solution and the Xenon lamp was turned on to trigger the photo-Fenton reaction. After 30 min, 200 μL of the mixed solution was evenly dispersed onto the surface of the solid culture medium and placed in a constant temperature incubator at 37 °C for 24 h. Similarly, control tests were conducted under the same conditions and steps but only with H2O2 and light (i.e., no addition of NiFe-MOFs or NiFe-MOF/CQDs). The antibacterial effect of the materials was judged by observing the bacterial growth.

By examining the TEM images of CQDs, NiFe-MOFs, and NiFe-MOF/CQD composite materials (Fig. 2 ), it is evident that CQDs are uniformly attached to the NiFe-MOFs. High-resolution TEM imaging reveals interplanar distances of 0.31 nm for NiFe-MOFs. In addition to the observed lattice of the primary MOF in the composite material, lattice stripes of introduced CQDs (0.23 nm) are also discernible. This suggests the successful coupling of CQDs onto the MOFs.

Fig. 3 shows the SEM images of M-SPVDF membrane surfaces grown in situ at 60 °C with different amounts of MOF seeds for different durations. When the seed content was within the range of 0 g to 0.15 g, the presence of MOFs on the membrane surface gradually increased with an increase in the growth time, while more NiFe-MOFs were grown on the membrane surfaces when certain amounts of MOF seeds have been pre-embedded in the substrates. However, when the seed content was 0.2 g, the MOFs on the membrane surface decreased with an increase in the growth time compared with the seed content of 0.15 g, and the growth of MOFs was affected. This phenomenon may be attributed to the excessive aggregation of MOFs on the substrates when the seed content in the casting solution was too high. This could result in insufficient sites for the in-situ growth, leading to an increase in surface defects.

By taking the growth of MOFs on the membrane surface into consideration, M-SPVDF-0.15/6 and M-SPVDF-0.15/8 were chosen for the incorporation of CQDs. The SEM images in Fig. 4 depict the membrane surfaces after the in-situ growth of NiFe-MOFs with the presence of CQDs at ratios of 10:1 and 100:1 ratio. Comparing to the pristine PVDF substrate, a layer of NiFe-MOF/CQD composites are observed on the membrane surfaces, Additionally, it can be observed that the growth state of NiFe-MOF/CQDs on the membrane surfaces differs from that of NiFe-MOFs alone, possibly because the incorporation of CQDs alters the crystal nucleation and growth processes of NiFe-MOFs [51]. The EDS elemental mapping images in Fig. 4e for MC-SPVDF-0.15/8(10:1) further confirm the successful growth of NiFe-MOF/CQDs on the membrane surfaces. Both kinds of nanofillers can be easily observed from the cross-sections by SEM (Fig. S1).

The FTIR spectra of the prepared materials and membranes are summarized in Fig. 5 a. The peak of Ni-O vibrations in FTIR spectra appears in the range of 510–550 cm−1. This characteristic peak shows a minimal position variation for the NiFe-MOF/CQDs, but the intensity decreases compared to NiFe-MOFs, indicating that a high CQD content may lead to a reduction in NiO bonds. No distinct characteristic peaks of NiFe-MOFs are observed in the spectra of M-SPVDF and MC-SPVDF membranes. This could be attributed to the lower crystallinity of the in situ grown MOF layer, consistent with the XRD results as shown in Fig. 5b.

The chemical characteristics of the near-surface region of membranes were further determined by XPS, with wide scans and C 1s core level spectra shown in Fig. 5c–f. Fig. 5c clearly displays the main characteristic signals of C and F elements in the wide scan spectra of the pristine PVDF substrate, while additional characteristic peaks of Ni and Fe elements can be observed for M-SPVDF and MC-SPVDF. The C 1s XPS spectra of the original and modified membranes are presented in Fig. 5d–f. According to the previous report [52], the C 1s peak of the original PVDF membrane can be deconvoluted into five peaks, respectively corresponding to CF2 (290.7 eV), CH2 (286.2 eV), and CC/CH (284.8 eV) due to the presence of PVDF and C-N (288.0 eV) and CO (288.9 eV) from the residual PVP. Comparing to the PVDF substrate, the M-SPVDF membranes introduce an OCO peak at 288.0 eV from the carboxyl carbon of the dicarboxylic acid ligands in NiFe-MOFs. As shown in Fig. 5f, a CO peak (288.3 eV) appears in the MC-SPVDF membranes owing to the oxygen functional groups of CQDs. In addition, there may be electrostatic interactions between NiFe-MOF and CQDs, which would cause slight peak shifts. For instance, the CO (289.1 eV) peak in the MC-SPVDF membranes has shifted towards a higher binding energy compared to the CO (288.9 eV) peak in the M-SPVDF membranes.

The surface hydrophilicity of membranes plays a crucial role in determining membrane performance [44]. Water contact angles of these membranes were therefore measured. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the PVDF substrate exhibits a relatively high water contact angle of 77.6° because PVDF is inherently hydrophobic. After doping with NiFe-MOF seeds, the water contact angle of SPVDF substrates decrease to 71.84°, indicating that the addition of MOF nanoparticles in the casting solutions improves the surface hydrophilicity of the resultant membranes. This improvement may be attributed to the hydroxyl groups (–OH) in the synthesized NiFe-MOF seeds [53], which can form hydrogen bonds during phase inversion and enhance the membrane affinity with water. The porous structure of the MOFs may also contribute to a higher membrane surface area, providing more surface sites for interaction with water molecules and thereby improving the hydrophilicity [54]. The growth of a NiFe-MOF layer on the surface of M-SPVDF alters the membrane's surface morphology and surface porosity, resulting in a higher water contact angle. However, once hydrophilic CQDs are introduced into NiFe-MOFs, a noticeable reduction in the water contact angle is observed, which decreases from 79.4° to 69.4° within 12 s, implying an increase in hydrophilicity.

Zeta potential measurements were employed to assess the surface charge of the membranes. As depicted in Fig. 6b, comparing to the negatively charged pure PVDF substrate, both SPVDF and M-SPVDF exhibit fewer negative charges due to the presence of positively charged NiFe-MOFs on the membrane surfaces. However, with negatively charged CQDs attached to NiFe-MOFs, the surfaces of MC-SPVDF membranes become more negatively charged compared to those of M-SPVDF [55].

The impacts of in-situ growth time on the flux and rejection rate of membranes with varying MOF seed contents are illustrated in Fig. 7 . Generally, comparing to the SPVDF substrate, M-SPVDF-0.05, M-SPVDF-0.1, and M-SPVDF-0.15 exhibit a decrease in flux and an increase in the rejection rate for antibiotics as the in-situ growth duration increases. This results from a higher MOF coverage on the membrane surfaces which reduces both the pore size and flux. The rejection rates of rifampicin (i.e., Rif) are generally elevated to 95% or higher, consistent with the high MOF coverages of the membranes as observed in the SEM images. However, the flux of M-SPVDF-0.2 increased and the rejection rate decreased when the growth time was extended to 10 h. This is due to the high crystal content in the casting solution, which causes MOFs to aggregate on the substrate, leading to uneven in-situ growth sites and thus increasing surface defects which is evident from the SEM images (Fig. 3). Considering the balance between membrane permeance and selectivity, the M-SPVDF-0.15 membranes with growth duration of 6 h and 8 h were selected for the addition of CQDs.

After the addition of different CQD ratios, Fig. 8 a shows that the MC-SPVDF membrane incorporated with CQDs at a ratio of 100:1 under 8-h in-situ growth leads to an increase in pure water flux from 85.0 to 147.5 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 due to the hydrophilicity of CQDs. However, its rejection rates of antibiotics drop significantly, especially for tetracycline (i.e., TC) which decreases from 82.4% to 23.2%, and the rejection rate of rifampicin falls below 95%. Interestingly, a further increase in CQD amount to a ratio of 10:1 would not only increase the NiFe-MOF/CQD content on the membrane surface but also enhance the intercept ability of large molecular antibiotics. Thus, the rejection rate of rifampicin jumps back to 98.1%, and the pure water flux increases to 119.7 L m−2 h−1 bar− 1 as compared with M-SPVDF-0.15/8. Considering both of membranes’ selectivity and permeance, the MC-SPVDF-0.15/8(10:1) membrane was chosen for subsequent tests.

To desalinate wastewater streams containing antibiotics, membranes consisting of appropriate pore sizes in the selective layer and a highly interconnected substrate are required to impede antibiotics while allowing the passage of salt ions and water [56]. MC-SPVDF-0.15/8(10:1) membranes demonstrate excellent antibiotic desalination performance since they maintain a high rifampicin retention rate of approximately 97%–98% and a low salt retention rate of around 1–2 % (Fig. 8b). The selectivity for salt/antibiotic separation exceeds 60, highlighting their promising applications for antibiotic desalination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation performance of Photo-Fenton catalytic reactions
ce:section.ce:para: Membranes with photo-Fenton activity can degrade antibiotics whilst their separation to avoid secondary pollution by antibiotics in the retentates. Here, to avoid the impact of adsorption by the substrates on accuracy of photocatalytic experiments, we used NiFe-MOF and NiFe-MOF/CQD powders to assess their photocatalytic Fenton activity to degrade rifampicin of 50 mg L−1 as a model antibiotic solution. As shown in Fig. 9 a, under dark conditions with H2O2, NiFe-MOFs exhibit a considerable removal rate of 35.5% due to the iron ions in NiFe-MOFs, which can react with H2O2 to produce hydroxyl radicals (•OH) to effectively degrade rifampicin. After doping CQDs in NiFe-MOFs, the removal rate of rifampicin increases to 50.1%. With the aid of a simulated visible light to the system, the photo-Fenton degradation rate of NiFe-MOF/CQDs is further enhanced to 84.9% while NiFe-MOFs only have 63.1% under the same illumination conditions. Based on the pseudo-first-order kinetics, the reaction rate constant (k) of NiFe-MOF/CQDs under visible light and H2O2 conditions is 1.65×10−2 min−1, which is approximately 85% higher than that of NiFe-MOFs (0.89×10−2 min−1). This proves the strong photocatalytic Fenton activity of NiFe-MOF/CQDs and endows the MC-SPVDF membranes with high potentials for the degradation of antibiotics and other pollutants.

To further evaluate their antibiotic degradation performance in natural water bodies with presence of various substances, water samples were collected from the Chunyang Lake at Zhejiang Normal University and used as the feed solution together with added rifampicin. As shown in Fig. 9e and f, the degradation efficiency of antibiotics in lake water by NiFe-MOF/CQDs is 99.8%, remarkably higher than that in DI water (84.9%), which may be attributed to the existence of humic substances in the lake water which can accelerate the electron transfer process in photocatalysis [57]. This finding suggests that the degradation of antibiotics by the MC-SPVDF-0.15/8(10:1) membranes in real water bodies is feasible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial performance of Photo-Fenton catalytic reactions
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned previously, the extensive production and dumps of antibiotics have also spread the antibiotic-resistant bacteria in wastewater harmful to human health and environments. The photo-Fenton antibacterial activity of NiFe-MOF/CQDs was thus evaluated by inactivating E. coli and S. aureus under visible light. Fig. 10 illustrates the results of the photo-Fenton disinfection with or without the catalysts. As shown in Fig. 10a1-a2, the control groups without any catalysts did not exhibit bactericidal activity, and the bacteria showed high resistance to the low concentration of H2O2 and visible light. Under the same conditions, a decrease in the populations of E. coli and S. aureus was observed when NiFe-MOFs were used as the catalysts (Fig. 10b1 and b2). In contrast, significantly fewer bacterial colonies were noticed on the plates in Fig. 10c1 and c2, indicating a clear inhibitory effect on E. coli and S. aureus by NiFe-MOF/CQDs. This antibacterial function may be useful for the removal of biofouling on membranes, which is also a serious issue for real applications of membrane technology. The antibacterial capability of the NiFe-MOF/CQDs may originate from several factors: Firstly, CQDs can absorb light energy and generate reactive oxygen species, which possess bactericidal properties by damaging microbial cell membranes or nucleic acids, leading to bacterial death [46]. Secondly, the presence of nickel and iron in NiFe-MOFs may lead to the generation of active substances with antibacterial properties. They could interfere with the normal functions of microbial cell membranes, thereby achieving disinfection [58]. Additionally, a synergistic effect between NiFe-MOFs and CQDs might promote the generation of reactive oxygen species under the action of photo-Fenton reactions [59], which will be introduced in the later section.

To elucidate the photo-Fenton mechanisms of NiFe-MOF/CQD composites, the following free radical scavenging experiments were conducted. Potassium bromate (KBrO3), formic acid (FA), and isopropanol (IPA) were respectively used as scavengers for •O2 −, h+, and •OH free radicals. As shown in Fig. 11 a, after adding each scavenger for 100 min, the degradation rates decreased from 84.9% to 58.5% (KBrO3), 63.4% (FA), and 56.1% (IPA), respectively, indicating that •O2 −, h+, and •OH all play roles in the photo-Fenton system. The order of influence of these substances on the photo-Fenton catalytic degradation efficiency of rifampicin by NiFe-MOF/CQDs is •OH > •O2 − > h+.

Transient photocurrent (TPC) responses of NiFe-MOFs, CQDs, and their composites under visible light were also tested. It is known that the stronger the photocurrent response, the higher the efficiency of photoelectrons (e−)-holes (h+) separation and transfer [60,61]. As shown in Fig. 11b, the NiFe-MOF/CQD composites exhibite a stronger photocurrent response signal than the individual NiFe-MOFs and CQDs, indicating that CQDs help promote the photo-induced charge transfer efficiency of NiFe-MOFs.Furthermore, the active substances generated in CQDs, NiFe-MOFs, and NiFe-MOF/CQD composites were identified using ESR technology and the production of free radicals with/without H2O2 and visible light was tested (Fig. 11c and d). Almost no •O2 − and •OH were produced before light exposure and the addition of H2O2. After the addition of H2O2 and light exposure, the signals of DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2 − were enhanced, indicating that the free radicals in the system were mainly generated by the decomposition of H2O2 under visible light by NiFe-MOF/CQDs.

Based on the above results, a possible photo-Fenton mechanism of the NiFe-MOF/CQDs is proposed. Firstly, under the light exposure, NiFe-MOF/CQDs are excited to generate e− and h+. The generated h+ can directly oxidize pollutants, while e− reacts with O2 in NiFe-MOF/CQDs to produce active •O2 −, simultaneously promoting the decomposition of H2O2 to form •OH. The generated •O2 − and •OH oxidize and decompose pollutants on the surfaces of NiFe-MOF/CQDs. The proposed reactions are as follows:
NiFe-MOF/CQDs + hv → e−+h+
H2O2 + e−→•OH+OH−
e−+O2→•O2 −
Fe3++H2O2 → Fe2++HO2•+H+
Fe2++H2O2 → Fe3++•OH+OH−
•OH/•O2 −/h++Pollutants → Products
Additionally, the UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) was employed to analyze the light absorption capabilities of CQDs, NiFe-MOFs, and NiFe-MOF/CQDs (Fig. S2). It can be observed that NiFe-MOFs exhibit relatively strong absorption in the UV range (210–290 nm), while in comparison, CQDs show high absorption in the visible light region (400–700 nm). Therefore, the absorption edge of the composites shifts to longer wavelengths compared to NiFe-MOFs after the CQD addition, implying their stronger response to visible light and the generation of more e−/h+.

This work has successfully prepared novel NiFe-MOF/CQD membranes with high photo-Fenton activity using the in-situ growth method. The influences of seed content in the casting solution and in-situ growth time on membrane selectivity and permeance were investigated. Compared to the original PVDF substrate and NiFe-MOF membranes, the incorporation of NiFe-MOF seeds into the casting solution and the growth of NiFe-MOF/CQDs on the membrane surface promotes the membrane integrity and surface hydrophilicity, and hence results in an unprecedented water permeance (119.7 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) and a high antibiotic rejection rate (98.1%) for MC-SPVDF-0.15/8(10:1). Additionally, during the photo-Fenton reaction, NiFe-MOF/CQDs exhibit a high photo-Fenton capability for rifampicin degradation (84.9%), and exhibit an even higher degradation rate in lake water (99.8%). Furthermore, the NiFe-MOF/CQDs can effectively inactivate E. coli and S. aureus in the photo-Fenton system, implying their ability to sterilize antibiotic-resistant bacteria in wastewater containing antibiotics. Our findings indicate that the NiFe-MOF/CQD membranes have significant potentials in the development of photocatalytic membranes for wastewater purification, and environmental protection.
